slides:
Repuck Hockey

Broken sticks, too many pucks wasted and a better way to play on the street.

Product & opportunity

What we sell  and why it can work

Repuck Hockey is a consumer and team focused product line: street hockey pucks made by grinding broken composite sticks, blending the fiber with recycled plastic, and injection-molding a puck tuned for driveway and rink like concrete. 

We see a viable market because (1) hockey households already buy pucks and balls repeatedly, (2) sustainability narratives move product in youth sports, and (3) rinks and associations need a constructive answer to “what do we do with snapped sticks?” beyond the dumpster.

      4-7Pomfret hockey players broken sticks 
$380Avg. composite stick (club tier)
2 marketsFamilies + rink/team bulk

Player counts and spend are rounded illustrative figures for a classroom pitch, not audited statistics.

Mission

Why we exist

Repuck Hockey keeps broken composite sticks out of landfills by turning them into durable, affordable street hockey pucks  so more kids can keep playing and families waste less money on gear that still has life left in the material.

The problem

Hockey is a very expensive sport, and that cost keeps many families on the sidelines. Gear adds up fast and one of the biggest pain points is equipment that fails before its time.

$350–400Typical high-end stick price
Too oftenSticks break anyway

Waste worth fixing

Expensive sticks break more than they should for what families pay. When they snap, the usual answer is the trash but that carbon fiber and composite material still has life in it.

Repuck asks: what if those broken sticks were recycled and reused instead of thrown away?

How it works

Partner with construction and recycling groups to collect broken sticks, process them safely, and give the material a second life.

Step 1 Collect broken sticks
Step 2 Grind into usable fiber / particles
Step 3 Blend with plastic
Step 4 Mold into street hockey pucks

The mix is tuned so pucks slide smoothly on concrete ,  built for driveway and pavement games, not the landfill.

Disruption

Are we “disruptive”?

                               We are disruptive to the street hockey world

  • Supply-side twist: feedstock from broken sticks lowers disposal problems for rinks 
  • We only have a couple of competitors

Market research

Street hockey & training accessories (U.S.)

Our competitors 

Leaders & strengths

  • Franklin Sports — mass retail distribution, price leadership, licensed looks.
  • Bauer / CCM (adjacent) — brand trust from ice; cross-sell training items.
  • Green Biscuit & others — premium training story, loyal niche.

How we differentiate

  • Verified recycled stick fiber % on-pack + batch QR traceability (goal).
  • Rink collection — bins, league co-marketing, “bring a broken stick” drives.
  • Concrete-first glide — formulation notes for outdoor play, not just logo swaps.
  • Team pricing — 24- and 96-packs priced to win club orders vs. commodity imports.

TAM/SAM figures are fictional but sized to feel plausible for a student venture plan; replace with cited industry reports for a graded submission.

Go-to-market

How we acquire customers

  • right to consumer — starter 2-packs and 3-packs; targeted ads in hockey-dense ZIP codes.
  • Rink & association pilots — co-branded collection bins; coupon on-pack for first online repurchase.
  • Tournament & tryout season — booth + “trade your busted stick” for a discount code.
  • Creator / coach micro-influencers — paid product seeding (~25 creators in launch states)

Retention

How we keep customers

  • Subscribe & save light — “season pack” every 4 months for driveway programs (optional skip).
  • League loyalty pricing — volume tiers for coaches who reorder each season.
  • Take-back — return worn Repucks (mailer label) for 15% off next order; material goes back into lower-spec batches.
  • Email drills + local events — not spam: one useful drill video per month during outdoor season.

Unit economics

Projected cost per unit

Blended COGS per puck (materials + direct labor + machine time allocated + packaging), at steady-state monthly volume ~8,000 units:

$1.42Fully loaded  puck
$0.60Recycled rPET + additives
$0.21Stick fiber (procurement + grind)

(Estimate)

Pricing & volume

Retail price & sales plan

MSRP: single puck $6.49; 3-pack $16.99 (~$5.66 per puck); team 24-pack $118 (~$4.92 per puck). Blended ASP after discounts modeled at $5.55.

Period (steady ramp)Units / dayUnits / weekUnits / month
Months 1–2 (avg)956652,850
Months 3–6 (avg)1681,1755,040
Month 12 target2851,9958,550

Assumes 7-day ops for production planning; retail sell-through may be lumpier than manufacturing.

Year-one sum of modeled months lands near $410k gross revenue (not compounded growth curve — stair-stepped by channel rollout).

Team

 labor cost

Roles at launch (mo. 1–6)

  • Founder / CEO — ops, finance, fundraising (partial draw).
  • Production lead — molding, maintenance, QC checks.
  • Part-time partnerships — rinks, recycling partners (~20 hr/wk).
  • Contract bookkeeper + fractional safety advisor.

Projected labor (fully loaded)

Months 1–3: ~$2,800 / mo (founder deferred draw, part-time prep + contract molder).

Months 6–12: ramp to ~$5,200 / mo (fits $18k operating cushion over six months).

Profitability

Projected margins by month

Gross margin per puck ≈ $5.55 − $1.42 = $4.13  Below is a simplified monthly operating view at month 6 and month 12

                                                                                           ( These are very generous margins )

Our goal

Turn the high-cost waste of broken hockey sticks into affordable street hockey pucks  so families save money, less composite hits the trash, and kids keep playing.

Repuck Hockey.  repurpose. replay. repeat.
